content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 24 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 112v–112r (LC PDF pp. 221–222) | **Source Images:** DEU_024_page_001.jpg, DEU_024_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 157–158 (pa_0157–pa_0158)

---

**1** When a man takes a wife and marries her, if then she finds no favor in his eyes because he has found some indecency in her, and he writes her a certificate of divorce and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house, and she departs out of his house, **2** and if she goes and becomes another man's wife, **3** and the latter man hates her and writes her a certificate of divorce and puts it in her hand and sends her out of his house, or if the latter man dies, who took her to be his wife, **4** then her former husband, who sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after she has been defiled, for that is an abomination before the LORD. And you shall not bring sin upon the land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

**5** When a man is newly married, he shall not go out with the army or be liable for any other duty. He shall be free at home for one year to bring happiness to his wife whom he has married.

**6** No one shall take a mill or an upper millstone in pledge, for that would be taking a life in pledge.

**7** If a man is found stealing one of his brothers of the sons of Israel and treating him as a slave or selling him, then that thief shall die. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.

**8** Take care, in a case of leprous disease, to be very careful to do according to all that the Levitical priests shall direct you. As I commanded them, so you shall be careful to do. **9** Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam on the way as you came out of Egypt.

**10** When you make your neighbor a loan of any sort, you shall not go into his house to collect his pledge. **11** You shall stand outside, and the man to whom you make the loan shall bring the pledge out to you. **12** And if he is a poor man, you shall not sleep in his pledge. **13** You shall restore to him the pledge as the sun sets, that he may sleep in his cloak and bless you. And it shall be righteousness for you before the LORD your God.

**14** You shall not oppress a hired worker who is poor and needy, whether he is one of your brothers or one of the sojourners who are in your land within your towns. **15** You shall give him his wages on the same day, before the sun sets—for he is poor and counts on it—lest he cry against you to the LORD, and you be guilty of sin.

**16** Fathers shall not be put to death for their children, nor shall children be put to death for their fathers. Each one shall be put to death for his own sin.

**17** You shall not pervert the justice due to the sojourner or to the fatherless, nor shall you take a widow's garment in pledge. **18** But you shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you from there; therefore I command you to do this thing.

**19** When you reap your harvest in your field and forget a sheaf in the field, you shall not go back to get it. It shall be for the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work of your hands. **20** When you beat your olive trees, you shall not go over them again. It shall be for the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow. **21** When you gather the grapes of your vineyard, you shall not strip it afterward. It shall be for the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow. **22** You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt; therefore I command you to do this thing.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Formal Equivalence Analysis, Deuteronomy 24**

**v.1 "certificate of divorce" (sefer keritut):** Lit. "a document of cutting off." The sefer = written document; keritut = "cutting off/severance." This passage is the foundational text for Jewish divorce law (get). LOGOS: "certificate of divorce" is the established formal rendering. LXX: βιβλίον ἀποστασίου ("book of separation/apostasy"), from which the concept of "apostasy" derives as a metaphor.

**v.1 "some indecency" (ervat davar):** Lit. "a matter/thing of nakedness/indecency." The same phrase appears in 23:14 for the camp purity law. The school of Shammai interpreted this narrowly (sexual unchastity only); the school of Hillel interpreted it broadly (any displeasure). The text itself is deliberately open; LOGOS preserves that ambiguity with "some indecency." LXX: ἄσχημον πρᾶγμα ("a shameful thing").

**v.1 "she finds no favor in his eyes" (lo timtsa' chen be-eynayw):** The standard formula for failing to please. This is the husband's subjective assessment, preceding the stated reason. LOGOS renders the formula literally.

**v.4 "she has been defiled" (hutamma'ah):** The Hothpa'al (or Pual) of tame' — "she has made herself unclean / has been made unclean." The defilement is the result of the second marriage. Returning to the first husband after a second marriage would be a form of ritual-legal defilement that would pollute the land. The logic is related to the Levitical prohibitions on priestly sexual irregularity (Lev 21). LXX: μεμίανται ("she has been polluted"), matching MT.

**v.5 "bring happiness to his wife" (vesimach et-ishto):** The Piel of simach = to gladden, make rejoice. The young husband's social obligation is to his marriage before any military or civic duty. LXX: εὐφρανεῖ τὴν γυναῖκα αὐτοῦ ("he shall gladden his wife"), matching MT closely.

**v.6 "upper millstone" (rekev):** The rekev is the upper turning stone of the hand mill; the lower stone is the rechen. Taking either in pledge would deprive the household of the ability to grind grain = deprive them of bread = threaten their life. "Life in pledge" renders nefesh hu' chovel ("it is the life/soul that he pledges"). LXX: μύλον ἢ μύλην ("a mill or a millstone").

**v.7 "stealing one of his brothers of the sons of Israel" (ganov nefesh mi-echav):** Lit. "stealing a soul from his brothers." This is the kidnapping/slave-trading prohibition. The law addresses what Exod 21:16 also condemns: the abduction of an Israelite for the slave trade. "Treating him as a slave" renders ve-hithamer bo ("and treated him as property/merchandise").

**v.8 "leprous disease" (nega' hatsara'at):** The broad Hebrew term tsara'at covers various skin conditions (not necessarily what moderns call leprosy/Hansen's disease). "Leprous disease" is the conventional formal rendering. LOGOS notes that the term is diagnostic-priestly (Leviticus 13-14) rather than purely medical.

**v.9 "what the LORD your God did to Miriam":** The reference to Miriam's tsara'at (Num 12) grounds the obedience requirement in historical precedent: even the sister of Moses suffered this disease as divine discipline. The verse is parenthetical but theologically grounding.

**v.10-13 Pledge laws — overall structure:** These three consecutive laws restrict the creditor's intrusion into a debtor's home and ensure the poor man retains his sleeping garment overnight. The structure is a graduated progression: (a) do not enter his house, (b) do not retain the poor man's pledge overnight, (c) return it at sunset. LXX follows MT closely throughout.

**v.13 "it shall be righteousness for you" (uledkha tihyeh tsedaqah):** The act of restoring the pledge generates tsedaqah — righteousness/right-standing — before the LORD. The formula echoes Gen 15:6 (Abraham's faith "counted as righteousness"). LXX: ἐλεημοσύνη ("mercy/charity"), which is a significant LXX shift from the MT's legal-covenantal tsedaqah to a charitable-benevolence frame.

**v.15 "he is poor and counts on it" (ki ani hu ve-elav hu nasa' et-nafsho):** Lit. "for he is poor and to it [the wage] he lifts up his soul." The idiom "lifts up his soul" toward something = sets his hope/longing upon it. The day laborer's survival depends on same-day pay. LOGOS: "counts on it" communicates the dependence; a more literal rendering would be "to it he lifts up his soul."

**v.16 "each one shall be put to death for his own sin" (ish bechet'o yumat):** The principle of individual moral accountability, enunciated also in Ezek 18 and Jer 31:29-30. This directly contradicts the common ancient Near Eastern practice of executing the families of criminals alongside them. LXX: ἕκαστος δὲ τῇ ἑαυτοῦ ἁμαρτίᾳ ἀποθανεῖται, matching MT.

**v.19-21 Gleaning (leket) triad:** The three gleaning provisions — grain sheaf, olive beating, grape harvest — are structurally parallel and triple-reinforced by the beneficiary formula "sojourner, fatherless, and widow" (ger yatom ve-almanah). This triad for vulnerable society members occurs throughout Deuteronomy (10:18; 16:11,14; 24:17,19,20,21; 26:12,13; 27:19). The gleaning laws are the background to the story of Ruth (Ruth 2). LXX follows MT consistently in all three provisions.

**v.20 "beat your olive trees" (tachovt zeytekha):** The verb chabat = to beat/strike. Olives were harvested by beating the branches with poles. "Beat your olive trees" is the literal rendering. LXX: ἐλαιολογήσεις ("you shall gather olives"), softening the mechanical process.

— LOGOS
